conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions.

Let's start with: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child - my son Alex. He's 8 years old. I'd like to know if I can claim Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I can help you determine if you're eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you a few questions. Let me start with:

**Does Alex live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't live with me. He lives with his mum.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. This is an important factor in determining eligibility.

**Do you contribute towards Alex's upbringing at all, for example by paying towards the cost of looking after him?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I'm not currently contributing financially towards his upkeep.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. I have one more important question:

**Is Alex's mum (or anyone else) currently claiming Child Benefit for him?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, his mum is already claiming Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, **you are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex**.

Here's why: Under UK Child Benefit rules, only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at any given time. Since Alex lives with his mother and she is already claiming Child Benefit for him, you cannot make a separate claim. 

Child Benefit is typically claimed by the person who is primarily responsible for the child - usually the person the child lives with. As you don't live with Alex and aren't contributing towards his upkeep, his mother is correctly claiming the benefit as his primary carer.

If your circumstances change in the future (for example, if Alex comes to live with you), you may become eligible to claim at that point.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£0.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.reason: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£0.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
circumstances: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£0.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

child_evaluations.reasoning: According to UK Child Benefit rules, only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at any given time. In this case, Alex lives with his mother, who is already claiming Child Benefit for him. Since you do not live with Alex and do not contribute towards his upkeep, you would not be considered the person primarily responsible for his care. The person who is responsible for the child (typically the person the child lives with) is entitled to claim. As Alex's mother is already claiming and you are not the primary carer, you are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (aged 8), who lives with his mother. The claimant does not live with Alex and does not contribute financially to his upkeep. Alex's mother is already claiming Child Benefit for him. Under UK rules, only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at a time, and this is typically the person responsible for the child (usually the person they live with). Therefore, the claimant is not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.
